fact: September 11 is exactly 254 days into the year, which means there are only 111 days left until New Year's Eve!
In the Northern Hemisphere, this date falls smack in the middle of spider season - house spiders are frantically searching for mates and scurrying into homes like tiny eight-legged party crashers.
The sun rises and sets almost exactly 12 hours apart around this time because we're racing toward the autumn equinox in just 11 days.
Maple trees in Canada and the northern United States start pumping out their sugary sap around this date, getting ready to create those jaw-dropping red and orange leaves.
September 11 sits right in the peak of hurricane season in the Atlantic Ocean, when these massive spinning storms have the most energy to work with.
Monarch butterflies are in full migration mode right now, with millions of them flying up to 3,000 miles south without ever stopping to ask for directions.
This date marks the beginning of rutting season for elk in North America - male elk start bugling like crazy trumpets to attract females and show off to rivals.
In the Southern Hemisphere, September 11 is prime time for baby animals to be born as spring kicks into high gear and food becomes abundant.
The zodiac sign switches from Virgo to Libra in just 12 days, making this date part of late Virgo season when people supposedly become super organized and picky about details.
Acorns are dropping like nature's bouncy balls around this time in oak forests, creating a feast that sends squirrels into hyperactive hoarding mode.
September 11 falls during the peak of the Corn Moon period, when the full moon closest to this date appears extra bright and orange-ish in the night sky.
Morning dew becomes super heavy around this date in temperate regions because the temperature difference between day and night starts getting dramatic.
